summary: This is the closing page of MacIntyre's Preface to 'Three Rival Versions of Moral Enquiry,' in which he acknowledges his intellectual debts to colleagues at Yale's Whitney Humanities Center and thanks the National Endowment for the Humanities for funding his historical research into the place of philosophy in the curriculum during 1987–1988. This research, he notes, directly underwrites the argument of lectures III–VII, situating the book's project in a concrete institutional and historical context. The page thus frames the entire inquiry as emerging from a specific tradition of scholarly community and institutional support — itself an enactment of the book's themes.

MacIntyre opens by distinguishing two historical conditions for philosophical lecturing: periods of stable consensus, in which the relationship between lecturer and audience rested on unspoken but deep agreements about subject matter and purpose, and periods of crisis, in which those agreements have been challenged or rejected and audiences have become fragmented. He uses the Gifford Lectures themselves as a concrete historical illustration, contrasting Lord Gifford's era (stable consensus) with MacIntyre's own moment of delivery (contested fragmentation). This sets up the book's central diagnostic claim: we are now in a period where rival versions of moral enquiry compete without a shared framework to adjudicate between them.

MacIntyre reflects on the reception of his Edinburgh and Yale lectures, noting that audiences did not hear them as neutral interventions but as contributions to already-ongoing debates within which they were already partisan. More significantly, he observes that the audiences were not merely divided but inhabited three distinct, mutually antagonistic frameworks of moral enquiry — so that the lectures were not episodes in parallel conversations but interventions in a continuing quarrel among incommensurable traditions. This sets up the book's core tripartite structure: encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomistic-traditionalist modes of moral enquiry.

MacIntyre observes that his lecture audiences at Edinburgh and Yale were already embedded in rival evaluative traditions — Nietzschean genealogy, Thomism, analytic philosophy, Scottish Enlightenment culture — and heard his arguments through those pre-formed allegiances, producing predictable disagreements. This multiplicity of interpretive frameworks is not merely academic noise but illustrates the core problem of the book: that the three rival versions of moral enquiry are not simply competing theories but incommensurable traditions that shape what counts as a valid argument in the first place. The page closes by raising the diagnostic question of whether academic communities are now so fragmented that any common address is impossible.

MacIntyre opens by asking whether the failure of philosophical traditions to communicate with one another is merely a sociological accident of university specialization, or something more deeply rooted. He identifies two key concepts — incommensurability and untranslatability — as necessary to characterize this second, more fundamental dimension of disagreement. He then begins sketching two opposed positions on these concepts, introducing the relativist view (associated with Kuhn) that rival large-scale systems of thought have no independent standard by which their claims can be adjudicated.

MacIntyre introduces two rival positions on incommensurability: those who hold that radically different systems of thought are untranslatable into one another's terms without distortion, and those (chiefly philosophers) who argue that incommensurability and untranslatability are always illusions — because recognizing an alien system as a rival already requires the capacity to translate it. MacIntyre then states that the lectures' aim is to show that admitting significant incommensurability need not make rational debate impossible; rather, such debate can reveal one standpoint as rationally superior on its own terms. The closing paragraph signals that in existing debates between opposing positions, resolution of first-order substantive differences has been indefinitely deferred — the problem the book sets out to address.

MacIntyre argues that debates between rival traditions of moral enquiry multiply into second-order disagreements about how to interpret disagreements, rendering direct engagement sterile. He identifies that the apparent theoretical impasse is not purely intellectual but is also sustained by institutional arrangements and social habits—particularly within the contemporary university—which reinforce the very philosophical commitments that prevent resolution. This institutional entrenchment produces ineliminable, continuing divisions across all humanistic disciplines.

MacIntyre argues that academic debate between fundamentally opposed moral standpoints is inevitably inconclusive: each position appears irrefutable to its own adherents and refutable to its opponents, with no shared rational standard capable of adjudicating between them. He finds it ironic that secular humanistic disciplines reproduce the same condition of incommensurability that their nineteenth-century predecessors used to dismiss theology as unacademic. He then begins enumerating four characteristics of contemporary humanities universities: (1) high technical skill on narrow questions, (2) promulgation of mutually incompatible large doctrines, (3) inconclusive public debate between those doctrines, and (4) a fourth characteristic cut off at the page break.

MacIntyre reflects on his experience delivering the Gifford Lectures at both Edinburgh and Yale, noting a persistent institutional reluctance to acknowledge that divisions between intellectual traditions run so deep as to constitute genuinely rival conceptions of rationality. He argues that what the lectures revealed was that the central question — what rationality itself is — cannot be resolved by appeal to any neutral standpoint, and that even the goal of presenting an 'overall view' of the contending positions cannot secure general assent. The conclusion he draws is that such lectures must aim not at agreement but at rendering disagreements more constructive, which is the explicit purpose of the published book.

MacIntyre opens by framing his Gifford Lectures around the question of what fidelity to Adam Gifford's actual intentions would require, arguing that Gifford's will — a document from a culturally alien milieu — demands that theology be treated as a strictly natural science, comparable to astronomy or chemistry. He notes that early lecturers like Muller and Caird shared enough of Gifford's cultural presuppositions to engage this seriously, but that later lecturers largely ignored the question because genuine fidelity would have been intellectually embarrassing. The key criterion Muller and Caird identified for natural science status is that its history exhibits rational progress in enquiry — a standard MacIntyre will use to evaluate rival traditions of moral enquiry throughout the book.

MacIntyre argues that despite Adam Gifford's mandate for natural theology to encompass enquiry into the foundations of ethics, the Gifford Lectures have produced not progress but a 'museum of intellectual conflict' — no agreed results, no consensus even on what rational progress would look like. He identifies at least three sources of this multiplied, apparently unresolvable disagreement: first, the absence of any generally accepted starting point or first premises among lecturers; second, the way arguments in the Lectures are characteristically organized to point toward rather than entail conclusions. The page thus sets up MacIntyre's diagnosis that the Enlightenment encyclopaedist hope for tradition-independent moral enquiry has structurally failed.

MacIntyre argues that philosophy's failure to achieve rational consensus stems from three structural factors: no agreed starting point for justifying beliefs, irresolvable conflicts over how to rank competing considerations, and the inadequacy of even the most rigorous logical resources to settle these disputes. He then catalogues the breadth of philosophical disagreement — spanning method, concept-assignment, meaning, mind-body, and the standards of rational superiority themselves — to show that rival schools supplement shared minimal rationality with incompatible schemes that cannot be adjudicated by any neutral procedure. This sets up his broader claim that the 'de facto unresolvable' nature of these conflicts parallels conclusions reached by historians and philosophers of science (Kuhn) and anthropologists (Benedict), pointing toward his tripartite rival-versions thesis.

MacIntyre argues that within modern philosophy, just as in the history of physics and moral anthropology, rival standards of rationality are incommensurable — so deeply so that it is not hyperbolic to speak of 'rival conceptions of rationality' both theoretical and practical. He then diagnoses a professional pathology among English-speaking philosophers: trained to treat all disagreement as in-principle resolvable, they deflect intractable disagreements through dismissiveness or assimilation rather than confronting them. This condition of systematic, unacknowledged philosophical fragmentation, MacIntyre notes, is the same condition already visible in the Gifford Lectures' fields of natural theology and ethics.

MacIntyre is establishing the intellectual context of Adam Gifford and his contemporaries, arguing that Gifford held a presupposition of unitary substantive rationality — the belief that a single, coherent set of rational standards governs all inquiry, including natural theology and ethics, treating them as sciences on par with astronomy and chemistry. To reconstruct Gifford's intellectual portrait, MacIntyre draws on a memoir by Gifford's brother John, a posthumous lecture collection, and Gifford's will itself. The broader point is that Gifford was a representative figure of a Victorian Scottish intellectual culture marked by democratized civic discussion of general ideas.

MacIntyre argues that while the Edinburgh establishment rightly objected to the arbitrary exclusion of certain data from enquiry in the Robertson Smith case, they made a further, unjustified assumption: that in all enquiry, the adequate identification and classification of data requires no prior theoretical or doctrinal commitment — that data 'speak for themselves.' This 'unargued belief' in presuppositionless enquiry, inherited from the philosophical influence of Reid, Stewart, and Kantian successors via Hamilton, forms what MacIntyre calls a 'unitary conception of rationality,' which Gifford and his circle took for granted as a universal feature of rational minds.

MacIntyre argues, drawing on Kuhn, that any mode of enquiry operates with both pre-theoretical and post-theoretical conceptual frameworks: the criteria by which data are identified and characterized are always shaped by prior theoretical or doctrinal commitments, not by neutral observation. He applies this epistemological point to theological and moral enquiry, arguing that what counts as relevant data depends on the enquirer's standpoint. This sets up the claim that Adam Gifford's hostility to religious tests was not a commitment to neutrality but was itself embedded in a particular theoretical conception of natural theology — meaning the 'no religious tests' clause and his conception of enquiry are internally connected.

MacIntyre establishes that Adam Gifford's intellectual world was shaped by the milieu of the New Speculative Society and the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition, edited by Thomas Spencer Baynes and later William Robertson Smith. Baynes is presented as emblematic of a Victorian intellectual synthesis — broadly educated, institutionally embedded, and committed to organizing knowledge within a 'distinctive architectonic of the sciences.' This architectonic, emerging in the late nineteenth century, subordinated all disciplines (biology, physics, etc.) to a unified framework of scientific transformation, making the Ninth Edition the canonical expression of the encyclopaedist tradition MacIntyre is about to critique.

MacIntyre, via his account of Thomas Spencer Baynes's editorial vision for the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, argues that the encyclopaedist project was not merely a neutral compilation of knowledge but a programmatic ideological vision: that comprehensive scientific synthesis would displace the Bible as the canonical cultural authority, subordinating theology and ethics to a unified architectonic of the sciences. Baynes's contributors were not monolithic, however — some, including Hamilton's daughter, were already moving in divergent intellectual directions. This page thus establishes the internal tensions within the encyclopaedist tradition even at its apex, and introduces the Ninth Edition's conception of science as individuated by subject matter rather than method.

MacIntyre, via the nineteenth-century encyclopaedist tradition (represented by Baynes and the ninth edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica), identifies four constitutive elements of genuine science: data/facts, unifying synthetic conceptions, methods of moving between facts and conceptions, and the continuous progress that results from their successful application. He then argues that this tradition held an understanding of science — both its internal details and its architectonic ordering — to be an essential part of general culture for educated persons, meaning there was no gap between professional and public scientific understanding, with figures like Agnes Mary Clerke exemplifying this ideal.

This page situates Agnes Mary Clerke within a Victorian narrative of moral and scientific progress, showing how the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition embodied Humboldt's vision of academic disciplines mirroring the cosmos's order, progressively revealed through science. MacIntyre argues that the human sciences added a fifth self-referential characteristic to the natural sciences — that progress of all kinds (moral, scientific, technological, theological) is their central subject matter — making the emergence of enlightened figures like Gifford, Baynes, and Clerke the climax of a law-governed history. The primitive, savage, and superstitious are thus cast as inferior precursors to this enlightenment, with figures like Frazer and Sidgwick providing the moral-historical backdrop.

MacIntyre situates Robert Flint — an unjustly neglected nineteenth-century philosopher — as the key theological contributor to the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, whose conception of theology as a science beginning from the data of nature and revelation aligned him with both his fellow contributors and Adam Gifford himself. Flint's central question — what constitutes genuinely scientific knowledge in theology — frames natural theology as a science still in the making, one whose method demands beginning at the right point or being forced to begin again. This places Gifford's project squarely within the encyclopaedist tradition's confidence in rational, progressive moral and scientific enquiry.

MacIntyre argues that the Enlightenment project of a unified, rationally contestable science of the whole has been radically undermined — not merely interrupted by external factors, but constitutively fractured by rupture and discontinuity. Drawing on Bachelard, Polanyi, Kuhn, and Foucault, he shows that the very history of rationality and science is itself a history of ruptures, making the assumptions underlying Adam Gifford's original project culturally uninhabitable. The central question becomes whether a systematically continuous and fruitful enquiry in natural theology is still possible, and MacIntyre suggests the answer is 'No' to the old form but 'Yes' to a radically renovated version.

MacIntyre sets up the three rival versions of moral enquiry by identifying the founding documents of two of the three traditions: Nietzsche's 'Zur Genealogie der Moral' (1887) as the charter of the genealogist project, which seeks to undermine the encyclopaedist's canonical ambitions and displace the very notion of a canon, and Pope Leo XIII's 'Aeterni Patris' (1879) as the charter of the Thomistic tradition, which calls for renewal of intellectual enquiry through Aquinas as a resource for critiquing modern rationality while preserving the canonical status of Scripture. MacIntyre then notes that Adam Gifford's will — the third document in play — understood ethics and morality as grounded not in human will or convention but in the nature and essence of the unchangeable God, aligning Gifford's project with natural theology. The page thus establishes the structural tripartite conflict that organizes the entire book, showing each rival tradition's self-understanding through its founding text.

MacIntyre identifies five key characteristics of morality as it functioned in Adam Gifford's Victorian culture: (1) morality as an autonomous, compartmentalized domain distinct from aesthetics, religion, economics, law, and science; (2) morality as primarily rule-following and ritualized response to transgressions; (3) moral rules as chiefly negative prohibitions, with condemnation attached especially to their breach; (4) a strong culture of propriety tied to compartmentalized social boundaries; and (5) widespread practical agreement on moral content coexisting with deep intellectual disagreement about rational justification, both underpinned by a shared conviction that morality must be rationally justifiable. This five-part typology sets up MacIntyre's diagnosis of what went wrong in Enlightenment moral culture — the conditions that made the 'encyclopaedist' project both plausible and ultimately incoherent.

MacIntyre, following Franz Steiner's analysis of taboo, argues that Victorian rationalists used the concept of taboo to simultaneously distance themselves from 'primitive' religious superstition and to manage the problem of irrational prohibitions within their own moral framework. By employing Tylor's concept of 'survivals,' they could classify residual taboo-like beliefs among Free Church members and Roman Catholics as primitive remnants, while framing their own Edinburgh bourgeois morality as the rational culmination of moral progress. This move reveals the self-serving circularity of encyclopaedist moral reasoning, which disguises its own contingent standards as 'timeless truths of morality.'

MacIntyre argues that educated Victorians, like the Edinburgh scholars of the Ninth Edition, were profoundly ethnocentric in their misreading of Polynesian taboo—projecting their own moral scheme onto other cultures while remaining blind to this projection. This ethnocentrism was compounded by a falsifying 'encyclopaedist' history of morality, exemplified by Sidgwick's Britannica article, which anachronistically read back the modern concept of 'morality' as a timeless, autonomous subject matter onto Plato, Aristotle, Hobbes, and Kant alike. The result was a conceptual flattening that obscured the historically conditioned and tradition-dependent character of moral enquiry itself.

MacIntyre argues that late Victorian moral culture faced two unasked questions: whether morality was susceptible of rational justification at all, and whether morality as they understood it was itself merely a 'survival' in Tylor's anthropological sense — a fragment detached from the larger context that once gave it intelligibility and rational grounding. Both questions converge on whether the moral frameworks of the Encyclopaedist tradition and its rivals are genuine rational achievements or irrational remnants of a broken tradition, setting up the three-rival-versions structure of the book.

MacIntyre argues that the standoff between Nietzsche's genealogical critique (Zur Genealogie der Moral) and the Thomistic encyclical (Aeterni Patris) reveals that both traditions, despite their radical opposition, agree in rejecting the moral world of Adam Gifford's Victorian milieu. He then advances a stronger thesis: not only are our conceptions of rationality and enquiry incompatible with Gifford's, but the very subject matter — morality itself — can no longer be understood as Gifford understood it, meaning any Gifford Lecture today conducts an enquiry into something fundamentally alien to what Gifford envisaged.

This page concludes MacIntyre's framing chapter on Adam Gifford's project by announcing the method of the ensuing lectures: MacIntyre will use Gifford's endowment as a platform to compare what natural theology would look like if guided by Nietzsche's 'Zur Genealogie der Moral' versus Leo XIII's 'Aeterni Patris,' thereby dramatizing the conflict between two rival traditions of moral enquiry. The page marks the transition from historical contextualization to the book's central tripartite rivalry. The underlined sentence signals MacIntyre's self-conscious acknowledgment that only Gifford's 'reckless generosity' in funding open intellectual inquiry makes this confrontational comparative project possible at all.

MacIntyre argues that the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica was the canonical text of a particular weltanschauung — a unified, progressivist conception of reason — and that the encyclopaedia article as a genre was perfectly matched to this worldview. He extends this claim to the university lecture of the same era: professors who wrote encyclopaedia articles also delivered lectures ex cathedra that functioned as 'spoken encyclopaedia articles,' a genre radically different from both medieval disputational lecture and modern academic discourse. The medieval lecture, by contrast, presupposed shared authoritative texts and culminated in disputation, grounding authority externally rather than in the lecturer's own rational synthesis.

MacIntyre argues that the contemporary university has made the nineteenth-century lecture format obsolete: where that format presupposed shared deference to authoritative texts and rational standards, we now inhabit a condition of radical disagreement in which a lecture can only be a moment within an ongoing conflict. This is why Adam Gifford's will — which prescribed lectures as the vehicle for conveying universally accessible 'real' knowledge — is doubly alien to us: both its content (encyclopaedist rationality) and its form (the lecture as authoritative performance) have become problematic. Nietzsche's abandonment of the professorial chair is presented as the historically significant rejection of both the encyclopaedist conception of knowledge and the lecture-genre that embodied it, anticipating his new role as genealogist.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche was thoroughly formed within the orthodoxies of nineteenth-century classical philology under Ritschl, whose project united philological, archaeological, and institutional study to render antiquity serviceable to modern culture. Nietzsche's rupture with this tradition—expressed most scandalously in 'Die Geburt der Tragödie'—stemmed from his perception that classical philologists had, in pursuing their academic purposes, actually alienated themselves from the classical realities they claimed to study and concealed this alienation from themselves. The outrage greeting that book, from Ritschl's silence to Wilamowitz's pamphlet, confirmed that the offense was not merely scholarly but struck at the self-legitimating structure of academic enquiry itself.

This page presents MacIntyre's account of Nietzsche's genealogical critique of truth, knowledge, duty, and right as masks for unacknowledged motivations and will-to-power. Nietzsche argues that to participate in the late nineteenth-century discourse of truth — exemplified by the Encyclopaedia Britannica's Ninth Edition — is to be a 'deformed person,' shaped by drives whose inhibition produces complicity in systemic suppression. The page identifies four key aspects of Nietzsche's mature thought (psychological, epistemological, historical, literary) already present in his 1873 essay 'On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense.'

MacIntyre examines how commentators have responded to Nietzsche's perspectivism — the claim that all truth-claims are made from particular standpoints — and argues that such commentary inadvertently smuggles in a universal, non-perspectival theory of truth to make Nietzsche's position coherent. He then raises the deeper problem: if Nietzsche's denials of Judaism, Christianity, Kant, and utilitarianism seem to carry unconditional force, his affirmations may too, risking restoration of the very conventional academic philosophy his genealogy was meant to subvert.

MacIntyre argues that the difference between encyclopaedic and Nietzschean modes of moral utterance is not merely about perspectivism versus unity of truth, but about a deeper contrast between utterance as fixed belief and utterance as a moment in a shifting play of forces. Crucially, Nietzsche himself did not fully surrender to his own genealogical mode — he retained a 'commentator self' that stood external to both modes, providing grounds for analytic anticipations others have found in him. The instability and ambiguity in Nietzsche's writing stems from his failure (or refusal) to demarcate a consistent boundary between this abstracted commenting self and the perspectival voice, leaving the question open whether such demarcation is even possible.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche deployed three distinct genres — academic treatise, aphorism, and prophetic/poetic mode — each as a deliberate philosophical strategy. The aphorism is not merely stylistic but constitutes an active, energy-bearing medium that outwits the reactive, academic mode of moral discourse. Crucially, Zur Genealogie der Moral poses a generic problem: it is neither aphorism nor poem but an academic treatise, and this is Nietzsche's chosen instrument for the genealogical task of exhibiting the historical genesis of the psychological and moral formations sustaining late nineteenth-century morality.

MacIntyre reconstructs Nietzsche's genealogical narrative in which the 'herd' morality of Judaism and Christianity — rooted in priestly resentment — displaced the aristocratic 'good/bad' polarity and installed a life-denying ascetic ideal that masquerades as objectivity, freedom from illusion, and scholarly neutrality. He argues that nineteenth-century academic historians, particularly French and German scholars, unknowingly perpetuated this ascetic ideal by claiming value-neutral objectivity while actually imposing their own institutional and teleological assumptions. The page closes by introducing Nietzsche's counter-move: his own narrative in 'Zur Genealogie der Moral' claims superiority over the academic historians precisely because it can expose the limitations and defects in their writing.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche's claim to the superiority of the genealogical narrative in Zur Genealogie der Moral can only be properly understood from within radically incommensurable standards — standards that his nineteenth-century academic contemporaries, committed to the unity of truth and reason, could not even entertain without finding them incredible and offensive. Their inability to do so was itself a symptom of their deepest commitments, not a refutation of Nietzsche. MacIntyre then pivots to introduce Nietzsche's epistemological foundation: that all theorizing occurs within a context of activity, and that different perspectives arise from different modes of participation in and response to that activity.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche, far from escaping dialectic, merely becomes 'one more dialectician' by mocking the pretensions of Kantian and Millian moral reasoning without turning dialectic against itself. The page then contrasts the encyclopaedist's unified framework — where truth is a relationship of knowledge to the world governed by rationality as such — with the genealogist's view that there are only perspectival truths, a multiplicity of perspectives, and no rules of rationality as such, only strategies of insight and subversion. MacIntyre closes by noting that the genealogical view requires a rupture from within the encyclopaedist tradition, understanding allegiance to that tradition as a symptom of inadequately managed rancor and resentment.

MacIntyre argues that the encyclopaedist and genealogist traditions are so mutually constitutive that no neutral third vantage point exists from which to adjudicate between them — any characterization from outside is doomed to failure. He proposes instead that one can learn each tradition's idiom 'from within as a new first language,' much as an anthropologist learns an alien culture, and that doing so reveals how each tradition's translations of the other's claims always amount to mistranslation. This leads to the conclusion that the two traditions constitute radically different and rival conceptual schemes, recognizable as such only by those who have genuinely bilingual competence in both.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche, in taking up the role of genealogist, had to repudiate the institutional forms of academic discourse — the lecture, the scholarly article, the magisterial treatise — because these forms presupposed encyclopaedic allegiance and could not contain genealogical content. Yet paradoxically, Zur Genealogie der Moral reads very much like a magisterial treatise, constrained by the same standards of factual accuracy as its encyclopaedist rivals, raising the question of whether Nietzsche simply relapsed into being a professor without a chair. MacIntyre then pivots to the genealogist's answer: that any piece of writing must be understood not in isolation but as utterance-in-movement, actively responding to what precedes it and anticipating what follows.

MacIntyre argues that the genealogical genre is necessarily open-ended, presenting theses about what has been in a form that cannot claim finality or fixity — unlike the encyclopaedist's ambition. This raises a paradox: the genealogist, by writing and publishing, implicitly addresses an audience and opens himself to being questioned in turn, requiring a kind of stance he officially disavows. Further, any piece of writing creates a 'shared now' between author and reader that transcends temporal separation, and the standards of reason-giving operative in that shared time are themselves timeless — making the genealogist's appeal to such standards an implicit act of metaphysics, however much he denies it.

MacIntyre argues that writer-reader encounters presuppose a minimal metaphysics: that both parties possess enough fixity of identity and relational continuity to enter into communicative relationships, even without sharing identical normative commitments. This 'modest, albeit metaphysical' claim is distinguished from Habermasian ideal-norm theories — it requires only logical, ontological, and evaluative commitments sufficient to recognize identities and differences, not agreement. The irony MacIntyre pursues is that post-Nietzschean genealogists who proscribe this metaphysics cannot eliminate its traces from their own writing practice, as evidenced by Heidegger's accusation of Nietzsche and Derrida's of Heidegger.

MacIntyre argues that the genealogist's challenge is to combine the fixity of particular stances (embodied in standard genres like the academic treatise) with the mobility of transition between stances — assuming and discarding masks without ever assenting to the metaphysical fiction of a finally true representation. He identifies Michel Foucault as the most impressive post-Nietzschean attempt to carry through this research program systematically. He then complicates the picture by noting that Nietzsche himself was not as alien to encyclopaedist conventions as Foucault and Deleuze assumed, pointing to Karl Jaspers's identification of a 'positivist phase' in Nietzsche.

MacIntyre argues that attempts to 'coopt' Nietzsche by translating his thought into conventional academic idioms fundamentally misread him, because Nietzsche's project deliberately subordinates elucidatory treatise to poem and epigram precisely to escape the constraints of grammar, logic, and established values — constraints the encyclopaedist tradition treats as necessary features of any intelligible discourse. The objection that Nietzsche is incommensurable with rational enquiry fails only for those who refuse to equate intelligibility with translatability into one's own conceptual idiom. MacIntyre is building toward the claim that Nietzsche's final standpoint must be approached from within, not judged by external standards, which sets up the genealogist's challenge to both encyclopaedist and Thomistic traditions.

MacIntyre argues that Nietzsche's writings cannot be adjudicated by neutral data, since competing interpretations rest on incommensurable standpoints — the disagreement is structural, not resolvable by appeal to the texts themselves. He then pivots to Foucault, whose trajectory from professor to genealogist intensified the problem of academic presentation as a mask, which Foucault addressed directly in his Nietzsche essay for the Hyppolite Festschrift. The page closes by introducing history's 'three uses,' all of which sever history from memorial veneration by generating a disruptive countermemory.

MacIntyre, drawing on Foucault's Nietzschean genealogical method, outlines three uses of history: (1) to expose official academic history as a parody that destabilizes fixed identities, (2) to comprehend persons as complex cultural composites rather than stable selves, and (3) to reveal in the historian a 'rancorous will to know' that demands destructive sacrifice rather than rational progress. MacIntyre then identifies a self-endangering paradox in Foucault's project — the genealogical insights that subvert official history equally threaten to subvert the genealogical project itself. He uses Les Mots et les choses as a case study, arguing that reading it merely as a Bachelardian account of discontinuities in the immature sciences (analogous to Kuhn on mature sciences) misses two key aspects Foucault himself foregrounds, including his interest in immature sciences as zones of obscurity between cultural codes and mature scientific order.

MacIntyre analyzes Foucault's Les Mots et les choses as a self-subverting text that enacts what it describes: it is organized as an ordered scheme of classification and representation while arguing against the possibility of such schemes. He then turns to Foucault's argument in 'Qu'est-ce un auteur?' that 'the author' names a role or function rather than a person, meaning the author's name assigns status to discourse rather than grounding it in personal intention — thereby ruling out any appeal to authorized intention behind the text. This move makes the text-as-authored a self-presenting unit, implicating Foucault in the liquidation of the conventional conception of authorship.

MacIntyre distinguishes Foucault's genealogical project from structuralism: where structuralists sought fundamental elements underlying all discourse, Foucault consistently denied any such foundations, instead tracing how 'epistemes' — the discursive practices unifying bodies of claims at any given time — are themselves products of power-laden assemblages. Sciences, far from being privileged access to rationality, are secondary phenomena whose apparent rationality is the surface expression of aggressive and defensive strategies; truth and power are thus inseparable. This page establishes the genealogist's core methodological claim that ruptures and incommensurable transitions between epistemes (as identified by Bachelard and Kuhn) reveal distributions of power rather than progress toward truth.

MacIntyre argues that the genealogist faces a self-undermining paradox: to carry out the genealogical project coherently, the genealogist must ascribe to himself a continuous, substantial self with deliberate purpose and commitment — yet the genealogical stance dissolves precisely such a self into nothing but what genealogy makes of it. The genealogist needs a persistent identity to move between masks and personae, but the logic of genealogy denies that any such identity exists beneath the masks. This page thus exposes a deep internal tension in the Foucauldian genealogical project.

MacIntyre argues that genealogy as a systematic project faces a self-undermining paradox: the intelligibility of genealogical enquiry requires the genealogist to draw upon standards, allegiances, and forms of recognition that genealogy itself aims to disrupt and subvert. He then diagnoses the history of genealogy — specifically Foucault's trajectory — as one of 'progressive impoverishment,' in which later work avoided collapse only by covertly importing non-genealogical sources and methods. This leaves genealogy currently resourceless before problems it cannot yet solve, though MacIntyre notes this gives no comfort to encyclopaedists either.

MacIntyre argues that the genealogical project, even if it has failed to fully overcome its own difficulties, has at minimum succeeded in impugning the encyclopaedist's form of moral enquiry. He contends that defects in genealogy's treatment of incommensurability among rival moral, scientific, physical, and theological standpoints do not diminish the force of that incommensurability itself — the moral enquirer is transformed from a neutral encyclopaedic participant into an engaged partisan of one standpoint against rivals. This dissolution is visible in the Fifteenth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica, where Mortimer Adler's editorial faith in the unity of knowledge is exposed as mere fideism increasingly at odds with contemporary intellectual fragmentation.

This is the closing passage of the chapter 'Genealogies and Subversions' (page 57, not 62 as stated). MacIntyre concludes his comparative framing of the encyclopaedist and genealogist modes of moral enquiry by asserting that the adequacy of his critique depends entirely on whether a third alternative exists beyond these two rivals. He announces that the next four lectures will be devoted to investigating the possibility of precisely such a third alternative — which the reader understands will be the Thomistic tradition-constituted enquiry.

MacIntyre argues that despite their radical opposition, the encyclopaedist and the genealogist share a crucial hidden agreement: both conceive of the history of philosophy from Socrates to the nineteenth century as a unified history — the encyclopaedist as a history of rational progress, the genealogist as a history of reason's disguised service to the will to power. This shared commitment to a unified historical narrative puts both traditions at odds with any view that sees philosophy's history as fundamentally broken or divided. The page thus sets up MacIntyre's own tradition-based view as a third alternative that rejects this shared assumption.

MacIntyre argues that Kleutgen's key contribution was not merely his particular characterization of pre-modern versus modern philosophy, but his insistence on rupture where both encyclopaedists and genealogists had seen continuity. The chapter then pivots to show that Descartes functions as a symbol for the encyclopaedist's ideal of reason liberated from any particular moral or religious community — an impersonal, universal, disinterested rationality. MacIntyre identifies this as the shared premise that encyclopaedist and genealogist both accept, framing an either/or: reason is universal and disinterested, or it is merely a mask for particular interests — and this false dichotomy conceals the Thomist third possibility.

MacIntyre argues that entering the Socratic dialectic already presupposes certain moral virtues and commitments, meaning the conclusions of Platonic-Aristotelian enquiry are partially predetermined by the initial moral stance of the enquirer. Nietzsche recognized this entrapment and therefore rejected the dialectic altogether, preferring the sophists — but MacIntyre notes that Plato himself was equally in conflict with the encyclopaedist assumption that truth is standpoint-independent and discoverable by any adequately intelligent person. The page thus sets up a three-way tension: the tradition-based enquiry of Plato/Aristotle, Nietzsche's genealogical rejection, and the encyclopaedist's pretension to neutral rationality.

MacIntyre argues that philosophy, properly understood in the tradition running from Socrates through Aquinas, is a techne — a craft — requiring both intellectual and moral virtues, with the master-craftsman possessing sophia as its telos. He contends that this craft-conception of philosophical enquiry, shared by the medieval liberal arts tradition (ars liberalis), stands in sharp contrast to modern encyclopaedic and genealogical rivals. The page then begins to unpack a key structural distinction within any craft: the difference between what genuinely is good to do and what merely seems good to a particular apprentice.

MacIntyre argues that the apprentice in any craft must learn to identify his or her own defects — corruptions of desire, taste, habit, and judgment — and understand what vices must be eradicated and what virtues cultivated in order to progress toward the craft's telos. He then extends this to moral enquiry specifically: because all rational powers (techné included) can be directed toward contrary ends, something beyond skill is needed — orexis or prohairesis, desire guided by right reason — to orient the exercise of the craft toward genuine good. The telos of moral enquiry is excellence in achieving the genuinely human good for the particular agent in his or her particular circumstances.

MacIntyre argues that moral enquiry is a virtue-guided craft requiring both theoretical understanding and practical embodiment, but this generates an apparent paradox (a version of Plato's Meno problem): one cannot develop the virtues needed for moral enquiry without already possessing some of those virtues. The resolution, partially drawn from the Meno, is that we have an innate potentiality toward moral truth, but we also require a teacher whose authority we must initially accept on faith within a community. From this emerges a conception of 'rational teaching authority' internal to the practice of moral enquiry, analogous to the master-craftsman/apprentice relationship in trades like furniture-making and fishing.

MacIntyre argues that standards of achievement within any craft or intellectual enquiry are historically constituted and justified — they emerge from criticism of predecessors, are guided by a conception of a 'finally perfected' telos, and are rationally justified as 'the best so far.' The schemes of deductively ordered sciences are themselves justified not by foundationalist certainty but by their ability to outperform rival competitors in organizing experience and supplying correction. The temporal reference of reasoning within a craft is thus fundamentally different from both the encyclopaedist's ahistorical rationalism and the genealogist's radical skepticism.

MacIntyre contrasts the encyclopaedist's conception of rationality — timeless, universal, tradition-independent — with the rationality of a craft tradition, which is historically situated, inseparable from the particular tradition through which it was achieved, and yet still aims at a timeless, perfected formulation of its own standards. He argues that to share in craft rationality requires sharing in the contingencies of its history and finding oneself as a character in its enacted dramatic narrative. The page closes by introducing the authority of a master within a craft as something more than merely exemplifying best standards — it involves knowing how to go further and directing others toward further progress.

MacIntyre contrasts three rival stances toward moral enquiry — the encyclopaedist, the genealogist, and the craft-tradition — by showing how each relates differently to authority, historical situatedness, and rationality. The genealogist (Nietzschean and post-Nietzschean) reads tradition as a mask for domination and Christianity as a purveyor of illusions, while the craft-tradition holds that reason-giving is genuinely truth-tracking even if historically situated. The page ends by identifying the genealogist's deepest target: not merely Christian ethics but the metaphysical realism — the view that the world is what it is independently of human willing — that underlies Jewish and Christian theism.

MacIntyre argues that the genealogist's accusation cuts deeper than simply charging theism with falsity: the very conception of rationality, language, and realism that the encyclopaedist employs is itself covertly theological, such that abandoning God requires abandoning the grammar of realist thought (Nietzsche's point in Götzendämmerung). Consequently, the genealogical challenge cannot be met by a nonrational fideism (like Kierkegaard's) but required looking back to a tradition-directed understanding of philosophy as techné — specifically Aquinas's account of sapientia in the Summa Contra Gentiles and the Metaphysics, where wisdom is explained through a hierarchy of crafts ordered to higher ends.

MacIntyre argues that the 'whole epistemological turn' of post-Cartesian philosophy is the outcome of a mistake — that the philosophy of craft-tradition exposes epistemology as secondary to the objects of knowledge, not primary. He then transitions to showing how late nineteenth-century theologians, unaware of the genealogical challenge, were nonetheless forced to respond to repeated attempts within Christian denominations to reshape doctrine to fit post-Enlightenment encyclopaedist culture, which provoked a variety of theological restatements — notably those of Kierkegaard and Newman.

MacIntyre traces how Antonio Rosmini Serbati (1797–1855) attempted to defend Augustinian and Thomistic positions against Kantian critics, but in doing so absorbed substantial Kantian structures into his own system. Rosmini's central thesis—that our knowledge consists in more than awareness of particulars because we bring an a priori universal idea of being to understanding—locates the idea of being as neither a product of nor property of the knowing subject, but as a 'given' whose givenness must be referred to God's action. The page argues that Rosmini's project ultimately failed on its own terms: either he failed philosophically by introducing intuitive apprehension of God into Kantian structures, or he failed theologically by making God immanent within Kantian cognitive structures.

MacIntyre argues that Rosmini's attempt to reconcile Catholic theology with post-Kantian modernity failed on both fronts: by accommodating Kantian philosophical standards, his thought ceased to be authentically Catholic, and by remaining Catholic, it failed to meet modern philosophical standards. This double failure made Rosmini a forerunner of Catholic modernism and much fashionable post-Vatican II Catholic thought. The Encyclopaedia Britannica's sympathetic treatment of Rosmini (unlike its hostile treatment of Aquinas and Scholasticism) is explained by his Kantian affinities, which aligned him with the encyclopaedist project.

MacIntyre argues that the neo-Thomist revival predates Leo XIII's 1879 encyclical Aeterni Patris by roughly thirty years, tracing it through a network of Jesuit educators — particularly Taparelli d'Azeglio, Serafino Sordi, and the Pecci brothers — whose institutional influence in Perugia and Rome quietly rehabilitated Aquinas against the dominant post-Kantian idealism. The political success of Thomism under Leo XIII was, in an important sense, premature relative to its intellectual consolidation. This genealogy of institutional formation demonstrates that traditions are carried and transmitted through concrete persons and educational structures, not merely through texts or official decrees.

MacIntyre contrasts Aquinas's and Kleutgen's understandings of the Thomistic tradition: Aquinas treats enquiry as an ongoing, collaborative, historically embedded enterprise — drawing on Augustinian and Aristotelian sources toward a single continuing enquiry — whereas Kleutgen misreads Aquinas through a Suarezian lens, presenting him as offering a finished, self-contained system. The deeper philosophical consequence is that Suarez's epistemology severs the mind's apprehension of universals from actual singular existence, a problem Aquinas did not have because his account of truth was embedded in a living tradition of enquiry rather than a static system.

MacIntyre argues that the reception of Aeterni Patris was fatally shaped by Kleutgen's prior epistemologizing of Thomism: those who responded to the encyclical followed Kleutgen in making epistemological concerns central, thereby dooming Thomism to the same fragmentation that afflicts all post-Cartesian philosophies — the indefinite multiplication of disagreement. Giovanni Cornoldi (1822–1892) had already anticipated this danger by contrasting Thomism's integrative unity with the falling-apart of modern philosophy from Descartes onward. MacIntyre's verdict is that Thomism, by epistemologizing itself after Aeterni Patris, unwittingly re-enacted the very disagreements it was meant to overcome, generating multiple rival systematic Thomisms each contending with different erroneous tendencies.

MacIntyre surveys how different Thomist schools (Mercier/Louvain, Rousselot, Maritain) each responded to their particular secular philosophical rivals, showing that Thomistic interpretation was always inseparable from philosophical apologetics. He then focuses on Maritain's attempt to ground human rights in Aquinas's natural law framework, arguing this was a fundamental error: for Aquinas, law is primary and rights are derivative from divine law, whereas Enlightenment modernity reverses this priority, making rights prior to law. MacIntyre treats Maritain's UN Declaration project as a 'quixotic' and ultimately incoherent assimilation of Thomism to a liberal rights framework alien to Aquinas's actual thought.

MacIntyre argues that the Thomistic tradition, represented by historical scholars recovering Aquinas's own developing thought, was challenged by modern epistemological and genealogical modes of enquiry. He contends that restoring Aquinas's thought requires confronting these rival modes not thesis-by-thesis but at the level of their overall 'modes of enquiry' — the craft-tradition of dialectical reasoning stemming from Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle. The true conflict between Thomism and modernity is therefore structural and methodological, not merely doctrinal.

MacIntyre argues that the encyclopaedist's narrative structure is governed by belief in the progress of reason, where individual lives gain meaning only insofar as they contribute to or obstruct that progress — canonization being the encyclopaedia's reward for contributors. He uses Henry Sidgwick's admission of 'chaos' in ethics (independent, incompatible fundamental principles) as the internal crisis that exposes the encyclopaedist tradition's failure: reason, on its own terms, discovers not cosmos but incoherence. The genealogist then exploits this appeal to timeless rational principles by revealing its function as concealment of a tradition-laden past.

MacIntyre contrasts three narrative stances toward the past: the encyclopaedist reduces it to mere prologue, the genealogist struggles against it as hidden rationality, while the Thomist treats the past as a source from which we must learn in order to move toward our telos. The Thomist reappropriation operates at two interrelated levels — theoretical enquiry (a sequenced progression of questions building on prior answers) and the practical embodiment of that enquiry — making the reader of Aquinas a participant in an ongoing narrative of moral inquiry. This inquiry, on the Thomistic view, discloses answers to foundational questions about telos, right action, virtues, and laws.

MacIntyre argues that the moral life is not merely theoretical but is embedded in the narratives of particular communities — farmers, craftsmen, families, cities — whose traditions of moral, religious, and intellectual life constitute the living context of moral enquiry. He then makes the pivotal claim that modern moral philosophy has been 'blind' to the complementary relationship between narrative and theory: until we can specify what enacted narrative a moral theory corresponds to in actual social life, we do not yet understand that theory adequately. The page closes by framing the three rival versions — encyclopaedic, genealogical, and Thomistic — as not merely competing moral theories but competing projects for constructing rival moral narratives.

MacIntyre argues that the superiority of one narrative tradition over its rivals is demonstrated by its capacity to incorporate and retell competing narratives as episodes within its own larger story — not merely to refute them, but to account for the very telling of those rival stories. He then pivots to the specific case of Aquinas, arguing that Aquinas's distinctive philosophical achievement lay in his craft-skill of integrating two deeply antagonistic traditions — Augustinianism and Aristotelianism — and that this integration was not incidental but constitutive of Thomistic moral enquiry. The Thomistic project, MacIntyre insists, must be understood as a response to conflict, and any adequate account of it must begin by examining what those two warring traditions actually claimed.

MacIntyre argues that in medieval Augustinian culture, the relationship between reader and text is reciprocally constitutive: the reader interprets the text, but the text also interprets and transforms the reader. This creates a version of the Meno paradox — you cannot read the texts aright until you are transformed, but you cannot be transformed until you read them aright — which requires resolving through obedient trust in a teacher and a tradition of interpretive commentary. The pre-rational reordering of the self must precede the cognitive capacity to recognize good reasons, meaning moral formation is logically prior to moral reasoning.

MacIntyre argues that for Augustine, reading sacred Scripture is not merely an intellectual act but a transformative one in which the reader discovers their own life re-enacted in the narrative the text speaks — the paradigmatic record of this being Augustine's own Confessions. He then examines whether Augustine's doctrine of interpretation extended to nonsacred texts, noting that the powers of discrimination required to judge secular literature rightly can themselves become an expression of distinctively Christian virtues. Finally, MacIntyre begins to argue that the relationship of reader to both sacred and secular texts is mediated by a tradition of teaching and initiation into which no merely human teacher can on their own successfully initiate a learner.

MacIntyre reconstructs Augustine's Platonic-derived epistemology, arguing that learning requires the mind to find within itself a source of intelligibility beyond itself — a movement toward timeless forms apprehended only in the light of God. Because the will is initially perverse and cannot naturally move toward the good, faith in authority must precede rational understanding, humility is the necessary first step in education, and rational justification is therefore essentially retrospective rather than foundationalist. The reading of texts mirrors this structure: movement toward what a text says and what it speaks generates a tradition of commentary that progressively raises the burden of dissent.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian/medieval interpretive tradition recognized that texts can bear more than one sense — historical, allegorical, moral, and anagogical — but insists that the historical sense is primary, with all other senses constrained by and dependent upon it. He then clarifies that 'sense' for medieval writers is not a property of isolated expressions or sentences but of units of discourse embedded in context, distinguishing this view sharply from Fregean and non-Fregean semantics. This sets up his broader argument that moral enquiry is tradition-constituted: meaning itself is not atomistic but embedded in interpretive communities and their practices.

MacIntyre argues that secular learning was integrated into the Augustinian scheme through reflection on the multiple senses of Scripture, a process that was largely unsystematic until the twelfth century. Hugh of St. Victor systematized this integration by insisting that the historical sense of Scripture requires secular knowledge (history, geography), the allegorical sense requires theology, and the tropological sense requires understanding of the natural world and human work within it. In this way, the doctrine of multiple senses of Scripture performs an essential integrative function, organizing all forms of enquiry within a unified framework.

MacIntyre surveys the heterogeneous philosophical inheritance available to twelfth-century thinkers: multiple Platonisms (Augustinian, Boethian, Pseudo-Dionysian, Islamic-Neoplatonic), partial Aristotelianism recovered through Boethius and newly rediscovered logical texts, atomism, and Stoicism. The key argument is that what prevented this diversity from collapsing into mere eclecticism was the existence of an overarching framework of belief — a tradition — within which these disparate ancient resources were assigned roles and put to work. This sets up MacIntyre's broader claim that rational enquiry is only coherent when embedded in a living tradition, not when conducted as a free-floating synthesis.

MacIntyre traces three converging strands of medieval intellectual development — dialectic, the quaestio tradition, and the making of distinctions (Distinctiones) — that coalesce in the comprehensive works of the twelfth century, creating new possibilities for radical intellectual dissension within an Augustinian framework. He uses Abelard as the pivotal case: a thinker who anticipated later logical developments but did so in the service of subordinating Platonism to Augustinian Christianity, not supplanting it. The lesson is that Abelard's radicalism was ultimately tradition-constituted, clarifying the relationship between dialectic and authority rather than escaping it.

MacIntyre traces how William of Champeaux attempted to transform Platonic realism into a theory of meaning for ordinary language, arguing that universals are distinct and separate from particulars and serve as the referents of common nouns. Abelard's decisive refutation of this position—by exposing its absurdities—cleared the way for a viable distinction between the intellectus universalis (the mind's everyday grasp of shared properties) and genuine apprehension of divine essences, thus rescuing the debate over universals from sterile nominalist-realist impasse. This sets up Abelard as an anticipator of Aquinas's synthesis of Augustinian Platonism with Aristotle, a move crucial to MacIntyre's genealogy of the tradition-constituted enquiry he champions.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian epistemology underlying Bernard of Clairvaux's condemnation of Abelard reveals something structural about tradition-based enquiry: the integrity of the life of enquiry requires submission to authority, because heresy is always a sign of pride — elevating one's own judgment above that of genuine authority. Authority is not merely an external constraint but an internal necessity of the Augustinian scheme, because rationally unjustified belief must precede understanding; belief accepted on authority is the precondition of moral and intellectual formation.

MacIntyre argues that within the Augustinian scheme, the initial acceptance of authority is not based on prior rational evaluation of evidence, but on trust in authorized persons — a trust whose apparent arbitrariness (e.g., Augustine hearing 'Tolle, lege') is only adequately understood retrospectively. By accepting authority one acquires a teacher who educates one into reading certain texts, and this process embeds one's personal story within a larger narrative of history moving toward intelligibility. Continuous authority is thus justified not as a permanent substitute for reason but as indispensable to a continuing progress confirmed by dialectical enquiry.

MacIntyre examines two rival modern views on the relationship between texts and their subject matter: (a) that texts are creative falsifications of their subject matter, so independent enquiry will reveal a lack of correspondence between text and world (Tolstoy, Sartre); and (b) that we have no access to a text's subject matter except through the text itself, making questions of external truth or falsity unanswerable. Both views, despite their mutual incompatibility, converge on the claim that no systematic correspondence between text and subject matter can be made sense of — a convergence MacIntyre uses to set up his broader argument about tradition-constituted enquiry and the Augustinian alternative.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition avoids the correspondence-theory objection by treating nature itself as a text — a series of signs authored by God — so that Scripture and the created world are both legible only within a tradition of authoritative interpretation. God is the ultimate interpreter of meaning, and ecclesiastical and secular authorities appointed by God are therefore authoritative in interpreting nature, history, Scripture, and the conclusions of dialectical enquiry. Progress in reading and understanding texts — including the text of nature — consists first in identifying discrepancies and incoherences and formulating hypotheses to resolve them.

MacIntyre argues that in the Augustinian tradition, theoretical and practical enquiry were inseparable, unified under the monastic ideal exemplified by the Rule of St. Benedict. However, in the twelfth century, new social pressures — from centralizing royal and papal administrations, changing occupational demands on clergy, and developments in logic and the artes liberales — forced the Augustinian tradition to provide a broader intellectual framework for new kinds of clergy and laypeople. In response, a new genre of comprehensive 'conspectus' writing emerged, exemplified by Hugh of St. Victor's Didascalion, which attempted to subordinate all secular learning to Scripture and practical perfection, echoing Augustine's own project in De Doctrina Christiana.

MacIntyre contrasts two medieval university models: Paris, an Augustinian institution where all learning was subordinated to theology and designed to re-educate students toward a more adequate knowledge of ends, and Bologna, where teachers were employed by students and education was oriented toward utility and political power, with law as its central discipline. He draws on Ferruolo's scholarship to argue that Paris's organizational forms and curriculum cannot be explained except as the outcome of a debate over educational ideals, not merely corporate self-interest. Paris thus represents an institutionalized Augustinian conception of enquiry in which theology governs the ordering of all other knowledge.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian conception of moral enquiry is not merely a historical relic but pervades modern thought in disguised forms: Descartes' cogito, Berkeley's sign-theory of nature, and Wittgenstein's account of ostensive learning all reproduce Augustinian structures, even when their authors believed they were repudiating them. Nietzsche's insight that 'we still believe in grammar' is read as an inversion rather than escape of the Augustinian standpoint — without God, there is no genuine objectivity of interpretation or conceptualization. The section closes by introducing Umberto Eco's critique of Porphyry's context-independent semantics, setting up the contrast between context-free and tradition-embedded meaning.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian scheme of moral enquiry cannot be evaluated piecemeal from an external standpoint: each thesis stands or falls as part of an integrated whole, and the scheme must be believed before it can be fully understood. This 'all-or-nothing' character shaped the form of the University of Paris curriculum, where each part contributes to a movement toward perfection encompassing both uneducated and educated participants. The uneducated grasp truth through scriptural images interpreted by the educated, making preaching and the university institutionally inseparable.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition, though it claims authority grounded in scripture and divine illumination rather than shared rational premises, is nonetheless vulnerable on its own terms in at least two ways: (1) an internal problematic inherited from Platonism concerning the metaphysical relationship between particulars and universals, and (2) the doctrine of divine illumination, which generates deep difficulties about how fallen human beings can access knowledge at all without grace. These vulnerabilities are self-recognized within the tradition, not merely external critiques, and MacIntyre uses them to set up his tripartite diagnosis of Augustinian/Thomistic tradition as a rational enterprise capable of confronting and resolving its own crises.

MacIntyre argues that the Augustinian tradition is internally committed to predicting the failure of rival rationalist projects — specifically those of the Enlightenment encyclopaedist, empiricist, Kantian, Hegelian, and positivist traditions — to vindicate their claims even on their own terms. The Augustinian must also claim that Adam Gifford's foundationalist assumptions (universally available, rationally warranted first principles requiring no prior religious commitment) are among the projects predicted to fail. This sets up a direct structural clash: Augustinianism requires initial commitment to a specific tradition as the only basis for genuine moral and theological enquiry, which is precisely what Gifford's will was designed to circumvent.

MacIntyre argues that Augustinianism requires not only internal progress in solving its own problems but also that rival traditions — particularly Nietzschean genealogy — must be shown to exhibit incoherence or resourcelessness by comparison. The rediscovery of Aristotle's philosophy in its full integrity posed a challenge of the first order to the Augustinian tradition, raising questions about key theological doctrines and the epistemological presuppositions of the Augustinian curriculum. The first specific challenge concerned the Augustinian claim — expounded by Hugh of St. Victor — that all types of secular knowledge, new or old, could be integrated within its scheme, a claim now stressed by the recovery of Aristotelian natural science through Islamic commentators.

MacIntyre argues that Aristotle's accounts of what a science is and of the telos of all enquiry were fundamentally alien to Augustinian epistemology, which had no need for such frameworks given its reliance on divine illumination. This created an unavoidable dilemma for thirteenth-century Augustinians at Paris: admitting Aristotle's scheme of studies meant confronting students with mutually exclusive claims, while excluding it meant undermining both the university's universal integrative claims and Augustinianism's own institutional authority. The chapter closes by insisting that before the Augustinian response can be properly told, the full dimensions of the Aristotelian challenge must first be evaluated.

This page (104) consists entirely of bibliographic endnotes for the chapter on the Augustinian conception of moral enquiry, citing secondary scholarship on medieval monastic culture, the twelfth-century renaissance, and the intellectual world of Hugh of St. Victor. The references point to foundational works on the origins of universities, monastic love of learning, and twelfth-century theological renewal, situating MacIntyre's Augustinian 'tradition' version of moral enquiry in its historical-institutional context. No substantive argument is made here; the page functions as a scholarly apparatus anchoring the chapter's claims about pre-modern tradition-constituted rationality.

MacIntyre traces how the challenge of reconciling Aristotle's doctrines with theistic belief in divine creation and the soul's immortality was first posed within Islamic philosophy before reaching Western Christianity. The Latin Averroists inherited their problems from Islamic and Judaic predecessors (particularly Averroës and Maimonides) but could not accept those predecessors' solutions at crucial points. Averroës' own dilemma — that unlike Plato, the Islamic philosopher cannot simply contrast philosophical truth with mere popular belief but must affirm the truth of mass religious belief — sets up the structural tension that will define the chapter's inquiry into rival traditions.

MacIntyre examines how Averroës and Maimonides each negotiate the relationship between philosophical enquiry and religious authority — for Averroës, the philosopher's faithfulness to Islam is judged by religious authorities appealing to the Qur'an rather than to philosophical rationality; for Maimonides, there is a crucial genre distinction between the Mishneh Torah (authoritative commentary standard) and The Guide for the Perplexed (rational philosophical enquiry), with Maimonides founding a distinctive philosophical genre for systematically reconciling philosophy with Halakhic authority. The page concludes by noting that for both thinkers, religious doctrines constrain philosophical activity without themselves being philosophically informed — a situation that changes dramatically for the Latin Averroists.

MacIntyre argues that the encounter between Augustinian Christianity and Aristotelian philosophy produced radical inconsistencies at three levels, the most visible being theological antitheses over the eternity of the world, the immortality of the soul, and resurrection. However, he contends that ecclesiastical authority's response was not blanket condemnation but selective restriction and partial prohibition, which paradoxically allowed Aristotle's works to be read piecemeal rather than as a systematic challenge to the whole Augustinian mode of enquiry. This tactical prudence by the Church delayed recognition of the deeper, structural incompatibility between the two traditions — a conflict not just over particular doctrines but over styles of thought, standards of rationality, and the very structuring of moral enquiry.

MacIntyre argues that even before 1240, the introduction of Aristotle's corpus into the Parisian curriculum was already destabilizing the Augustinian framework — not merely at the level of individual theses, but at the structural level of how enquiry itself was organized and how theology related to other disciplines. The deeper challenge was that incorporating Aristotle fully would have granted philosophy a relative independence from theology, transforming what had been a mere prologue to theological study into a self-sufficient mode of enquiry. This constituted a second-level challenge to Augustinianism: not just disagreement on particular questions, but a rival conception of the architecture of knowledge itself.

MacIntyre contrasts Aristotle and Augustine on three key issues: (1) the mind's capacity for knowledge — Aristotle sees the mind as self-sufficient in achieving knowledge, while Augustine requires divine illumination to compensate for an otherwise impotent mind, leading to the Cartesian problematic on the Augustinian path but not the Aristotelian; (2) the nature of truth — for Aristotle truth is located in the mind's relationship to objects, for Augustine it is located in God as the source of 'veritas,' making truth of statements tertiary; and (3) the nature of defect and error — for Augustine the will is the primary cause of error, the intellect being liable to perversion by a will uninformed by charity, so that Augustinian enquiry always suspects intellectual failure of being rooted in moral defect.

MacIntyre argues that the conflict between Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions at the University of Paris appeared to many as systematically irresolvable except by theological fiat, because the data used to adjudicate between them were already conceptually shaped by one tradition or the other — neutral data sufficient to resolve the dispute simply did not exist. He illustrates this through Siger of Brabant's double-truth strategy (philosophy's conclusions are 'conclusions of philosophy, but not truth') and Bonaventure's refusal to argue at the philosophical level at all. He then corrects Mandonnet's discredited 'double truth' attribution to the Latin Averroists, noting that no consistent Aristotelian could hold such a doctrine.

MacIntyre argues that the University of Paris debates between Augustinianism and Aristotelianism represent a classic case of incommensurability between two rival conceptual schemes. He establishes a key epistemological point: incommensurability cannot be recognized or adequately characterized by those who inhabit only one of the two schemes. Furthermore, any 'translation' project between rival schemes will either be judged a failure (rendering the rival unintelligible) or, if deemed successful, will necessarily distort the rival scheme by rewriting it into the idioms of the translator's own scheme, making the rival appear either compatible with or refutable from that standpoint.

MacIntyre argues on this page that the Augustinian theologians at Paris in the 1260s-70s were correct to treat Aristotelian conceptual schemes as genuinely incommensurable threats — not because they were 'Davidsonians' avant la lettre, but because incommensurability only becomes visible from a standpoint that refuses to pre-assume translatability. Genuine incommensurability can only be recognized by someone who inhabits both rival conceptual schemes simultaneously — a 'native speaker of two first languages' — who need not translate between them but understands each from within. MacIntyre uses Aquinas as the exemplary figure of this dual inhabitation, having been trained by Albertus Magnus in both Augustinian and Aristotelian idioms at Cologne and Paris.

MacIntyre explains how Albertus Magnus uniquely positioned his pupils to understand both Aristotelianism and Augustinianism 'from within' — as internally coherent traditions — by carefully separating his commentary work from his own theological commitments. Among his pupils, only Aquinas went further, immersing himself so fully in both traditions that reconciling their rival truth-claims became not merely an intellectual project but a problem of his very existence. Aquinas then systematically confronted these two apparently incommensurable standpoints at Paris after 1252, though MacIntyre hints the challenge was even deeper than a simple two-rival-thesis contest.

MacIntyre argues that every tradition of moral enquiry has a history with a starting point, a set of established beliefs and practices, and a characteristic way of facing challenges — whether from external rivals, internal incoherence, or discovered resourcelessness before theoretical problems. From that point forward, a tradition justifies itself by showing rational superiority to its predecessor, moving through stages where beliefs and practices are reformulated toward theoretical and practical goals. Crucially, the mere availability of historical reference backward is not sufficient to constitute rational enquiry; a directedness toward goals must also emerge.

MacIntyre argues on this page that the question of what constitutes the rationality of a tradition cannot be answered from a neutral standpoint — every evaluative framework is itself tradition-bound. He then makes a pivotal self-disclosure: he himself speaks as a partisan, and the academic neutrality claimed by the encyclopaedist is a fiction. Because one's presentation of any conflict — including the 13th-century clash between Augustinianism and Aristotelianism — is inescapably framed by one's own tradition's beliefs and purposes, rival traditions will generate rival histories of the same events.

MacIntyre argues on this page that what appeared to be incommensurable disagreements between Aristotelian and early modern scientific traditions — as diagnosed by Kuhn and Feyerabend — were actually resolved through the internal development of the scientific tradition itself. He challenges Feyerabend's claim that Galileo's victory was achieved through rhetorical manipulation rather than rational argument, contending that Feyerabend's assumptions about rational resources available across traditions are defective. The deeper point is that genuine reconciliation between rival traditions is possible, but only when understood from within a tradition that has the resources to represent and overcome its predecessor's failures.

MacIntyre argues that epistemological crises within a tradition can be resolved through 'imaginative occupation' of an alien standpoint — one imaginatively inhabits another tradition's point of view prospectively, then concludes retrospectively that the new scheme is superior. He illustrates this with Aquinas, who invited both Augustinians and Averroists to occupy his synthesized standpoint, which integrated and then corrected the defects of both Augustinianism and Aristotelianism as judged from within their own respective criteria. This achievement was possible precisely because the incommensurable differences between the traditions made mutual self-understanding from the rival point of view otherwise impossible.

MacIntyre argues that any scheme of belief and enquiry contains an ineliminable reference beyond itself — to truth and being as such — that cannot be reduced to warranted assertibility within that scheme. This requires a 'metaphysics of being,' of esse over and above what can be said about particular entia. He then shows how Aquinas, responding to this type of problem (avant la lettre, before Bachelard, Kuhn, Feyerabend, Davidson), began defining his distinctive position in De Ente et Essentia and De Veritate — works that function as philosophical dictionaries spelling out the analogical uses of 'true,' 'truth,' 'being,' and 'essence,' all anchored in God as primary analogate. The page culminates in the insight that to understand the analogical relationships one must reverse the generative causal order: beginning from the derivative (finite beings) to work back toward what is first in being and truth.

MacIntyre argues on this page that Aquinas achieved a genuine synthesis of Aristotelian and Augustinian frameworks by using each tradition's internal standards to correct the other's deficiencies, rather than merely harmonizing them. The Quinque Viae serve as the structural hinge: rejecting them would mean rejecting the shared conception of enquiry that both Aristotelians and Augustinians already presupposed. Retrospectively, Aquinas rescued both standpoints from imminent epistemological crises, showing that Aristotelian natural philosophy required Augustinian supernatural theology for its completion, and that Augustine's account of the human knower required Aristotle's rational framework as its prologue.
