MacIntyre argues that within Aquinas's scheme, particular theses are justified dialectically or demonstratively, but the overall scheme itself is justified by its capacity to remedy defects, transcend limitations, and extend the reach of its tradition. The 'conception of truth' embedded in Thomism commits its adherents to engaging any alternative standpoint — including encyclopaedist and genealogist rivals — by rendering those standpoints intelligible from within Thomism better than they can render themselves, exposing their defects and limitations. This commitment to truth thus opens Thomism to radical self-judgment, making intellectual vulnerability the mark of worthwhile theorizing.

On this page, MacIntyre is transitioning from his comparative critique of the encyclopaedist and genealogical traditions to a more precise articulation of what the Thomistic tradition requires by way of moral enquiry. He argues that the task now is to specify more carefully how both the form of moral enquiry and the form of the moral life are to be understood within the Thomistic scheme, and how that understanding relates — and has proven superior — to the rival claims of encyclopaedia and genealogy. This sets up the positive constructive phase of the book after the critical comparative work.

MacIntyre argues that mastering a craft requires learning two key distinctions: between what merely seems good to me and what really is good, and between what is good for me now given my limited education and what is good unqualifiedly. Crucially, these distinctions cannot be applied in a fixed, once-and-for-all manner because every craft has an ongoing, incomplete history that continually generates new demands on how distinctions are applied. Therefore, learning to make these distinctions adequately is itself a historical and progressive achievement — one must acquire a practical 'knowing how' that enables movement from past achievements toward deeper understanding.

MacIntyre argues that craft-traditions require the embodied transmission of a capacity to recognize, in the past, what guides the future — and that to be initiated into a craft is to be initiated into a tradition in good order. He then extends this to moral philosophy: the lives of craftspersons are themselves the subject matter of moral enquiry, and both the philosophical theorist (asking 'what is the good for human beings?') and the individual agent (asking 'what is my good?') are mutually dependent — no purely theoretical answer can be given that is not translatable into practical answers, and no practical answer can be given that does not presuppose some philosophical framework. Finally, MacIntyre introduces Aquinas's account of connatural moral knowledge — virtuous practice itself yields a form of knowing distinct from theoretical knowledge.

MacIntyre argues on this page that initiation into the moral life is simultaneously initiation into the complex history of moral enquiry — the two are inseparable. Drawing on Aquinas's account of self-education (from the Quaestiones Disputate De Veritate), he contends that all genuine teaching mirrors the order of the learner's own discovery, and that the Summa Theologiae enacts this by leading the reader through the narrative history of enquiry up to its highest achieved point. The Summa thus sets out a framework of universal moral concepts not as static doctrine but as a tradition to be rationally inhabited and enacted by individuals formed within it.

MacIntyre argues that to learn how to read the Summa Theologiae rightly, one must already possess certain intellectual and moral virtues — particularly prudentia/phronesis — which themselves cannot be possessed unless the moral virtues are already in place. This circular structure recapitulates Meno's paradox at the level of moral enquiry: we must already be, to some degree, what we are trying to become through the enquiry. The resolution lies in beginning with nascent, ordered passions and initial rule-following experiences, so that formal systematic moral enquiry (including reading the Summa) can build upon and refine those already-forming virtues.

MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae represents the highest stage of Aquinas's integrated curricular vision, in which theology provides the framework within which all subordinate disciplines — possessing real but limited autonomy — are to be understood. This position was in direct tension with the dominant Augustinian curriculum, which, by grounding all understanding in divine exemplars yet leaving the links between theology and other disciplines practically unexplained, had produced a de facto disciplinary fragmentation and loss of curricular unity. Hugh of St. Victor's Didascalion was an earlier attempt to restore that unity, but MacIntyre implies it was insufficient to resolve the underlying Augustinian epistemological problem.

MacIntyre reconstructs the institutional crisis at the medieval University of Paris occasioned by the reception of Aristotle's physical and metaphysical works: if those works were assigned to the Faculty of Arts, the Arts faculty would gain autonomous authority over questions previously governed by theology, yet if assigned to Theology, theology itself would have to become philosophical in a radically new way. Only after Albertus Magnus set new standards of Aristotelian presentation did it become clear that theology might have to become a philosophical discipline — a break with conventional Augustinian understanding. Aquinas's Summa then embodies this resolution: by integrating metaphysical commentary into theology's own argumentative structure, theology is no longer sovereign dictator over subordinate disciplines but must argue with them, producing a work whose genre separated it from both conventional orthodoxies and the Averroist program.

MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae is not a neutral text accessible to any rational reader, but rather requires the reader to be a certain kind of person — formed morally and theologically — in order to read it aright. This constitutes a direct challenge to the liberal assumption that every rational adult is free to read any book. He then introduces two contrasting ways of reading Aquinas's questions on law (Ia-IIae, qq. 90–97): one that isolates the text from Aquinas's broader corpus, and one that reads it within the full tradition — showing that the first method produces irresolvable disagreement because readers import alien philosophical frameworks.

MacIntyre argues on this page that Aquinas's questions 90–97 on natural law are not self-interpreting: they can only be properly understood after working through questions 1–89, because the earlier discussion of 'good' establishes that identifying something as good is a factual judgment about what moves a being toward its natural end. Evaluative judgments are therefore a species of factual judgment about the final and formal causes of a species' activity. The concept of good applies only to beings insofar as they are members of some species or kind, and our understanding of those goods is cumulative, developmental, and subject to error — meaning questions 90–97 represent a mature understanding that corrects earlier, albeit genuine, misunderstandings of natural law.

MacIntyre argues that the Summa Theologiae must be read as a unified whole rather than as a collection of extractable theses, because abstracting particular arguments — comparing them to Kant, Hume, or Hegel — fundamentally deforms Aquinas's thought. Contemporary curricular habits (e.g., publishing Questions 90–97 as a standalone 'Treatise on Law') perpetuate this deformation by inviting readers to engage Aquinas without first inhabiting the dialectical universe of discourse the Summa constitutes. A genuinely critical engagement with the Summa therefore requires first being a participant in its processes of enquiry and discovery, a condition nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism largely ignored.

MacIntyre argues that the plain person's knowledge of natural law is expressed not through explicit philosophical formulation but through the practical judgments they make, which presuppose the principium. Philosophical disagreements about how to formulate the principium parallel disagreements about non-contradiction — both require a web of interrelated concepts spelled out through their range of uses and applications. The movement from primitive apprehension of the good toward mature understanding requires engagement with concepts like telos, happiness, action, passion, and virtue, and this understanding is fundamentally communal and teachable, achieved through encounter with others in community.

MacIntyre argues on this page that the movement toward the ultimate human good must be initiated in the household and wider community through moral formation, and that Aquinas—working through Aristotle's own criteria—turns those criteria against Aristotle to show that the ultimate good cannot be found in any created thing: wealth, honor, pleasure, the virtues, power, or bodily goods. The Aristotelianism that results has a 'tragic character' because, without rationally warranted belief in divine goodness, there can be no genuine knowledge of the perfect good toward which the soul is oriented. Aquinas thus positions himself as a better Aristotelian than Aristotle by invoking Scripture and Augustine, not to abandon Aristotelianism but to complete it.

MacIntyre argues that for an Aristotelian (whether Thomist or otherwise), what is good for any being is determined by its essential nature and the flourishing proper to its kind. Virtues are dispositions to judge particulars in light of truths about one's species, and being practically well-educated means taking pleasure and pain in the right things. Crucially, once you remove the notion of essential nature — as metaphysical nominalism does — the entire Aristotelian scheme of the self, good, and pleasure collapses into a bare individual self with only its pleasures and pains, foreshadowing Hobbesian conclusions about endless desire.

MacIntyre argues that the discovery of sin and wilful evil — far from leading to Hobbesian despair or Averroist denial — is, for Aquinas, the very condition that makes the human end achievable, because it opens the agent to the theological virtues of faith, hope, and charity. Only the kind of knowledge faith provides, the expectation hope provides, and the capacity for friendship with God that charity provides can complete and correct what natural virtue and natural law left unfinished. The self-revelation of God in scriptural history is thus not a supplement to moral enquiry but its necessary retrospective completion.

MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's corrected Aristotelianism — supplemented by Augustine and Scripture — provides a richer account of the moral life than either pure Aristotelianism or its Lutherian rejection, especially through the cardinal and theological virtues (with charity as their form). A key structural point emerges: Aquinas holds that knowledge of God is not optional but foundational to moral enquiry from the outset, since the unifying framework of natural law — tracing formal, final, efficient, and material causality — always refers back to a unified first cause. This positions Thomistic enquiry against modern natural law theorists who claim we can understand and obey the natural law without any knowledge of God.

MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's moral philosophy is irreducibly political: the conflicts of moral life are bound up with conflicts of competing jurisdictions (university, episcopal, royal, imperial, papal), and it was through these concrete historical conflicts that universal moral truths found their highly particular embodiments. The individual moral life continues and extends the tradition that formed it, appropriating teachings from a variety of pasts, so that the rationality of individual moral enquiry is the rationality embodied in and transmitted through tradition. The Summa itself is a definitive theoretical statement of what that tradition has reached so far, with its sequences portraying possibilities awaiting embodiment in particular persons, circumstances, times, and places.

MacIntyre argues that the individual human being is a unity whose spiritual and social existence must be ordered hierarchically into a unified mode of life, yet each role (biological unit, family member, citizen, monk, etc.) carries its own importance and generates genuine tensions. The practical problems of integrating the self are the direct counterpart to the practical problems of competing jurisdictions in political community, and the virtues required for a well-integrated self are identical to those required for a well-integrated polity. Aquinas himself is offered as a living exemplar of this unity: his single-minded rejection of political entanglement and insistence on his intellectual vocation underwrote the quality Dante most admired — discrezione — the practical wisdom to make right moral and intellectual distinctions.

MacIntyre uses Dante's placement of Brunetto Latini and Emperor Frederick II in Hell to illustrate Aquinas's thesis that the badness of any part of a self entails the badness of the whole: doing good deeds is perfectly compatible with a perverse fundamental choice of self, and hell is simply the persistence in that defection. He then contrasts Nietzsche's praise of Frederick II's ruthless self-affirmation with Dante and Aquinas's condemnation of the very same disposition, arguing that what counts as achievement of the good versus emasculation depends entirely on which larger narrative framework — the Commedia's scriptural vision or Nietzsche's genealogical one — one's individual story is embedded within.

MacIntyre argues that from a Thomistic standpoint, rational justification operates at four distinct levels depending on the interlocutor's epistemic and moral formation: (1) the genuinely uninstructed person asking 'What is my good?', (2) the person already sharing the Aristotelian-Thomistic larger scheme who debates within a common framework, and (3) the antagonist who systematically rejects first principles, creating incommensurability. The chapter uses Nietzsche's admiration for Dante to show how a Nietzschean reading inverts Dante's moral intentions, which sets up the question of what Thomistic resources can resist such a genealogical appropriation. The four-levels framework is MacIntyre's structural answer to the challenge of rational justification across rival traditions.

MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's synthesis can be shown rationally superior to prior traditions by demonstrating that it can explain the limitations and errors of rival views in their own terms, while incorporating what is cogent within them. A key historical mistake of nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Thomism was treating the task of rational justification against Cartesian, Humean, and Kantian adversaries as a 'second-type' problem (shared first principles) rather than a 'third-type' problem (tradition-constituted rationality from incommensurable starting points). Against Nietzschean opponents especially, this error is compounded because Nietzsche reveals how a philosophical tradition can be so institutionally organized that its adherents become systematically blind to their own errors.

MacIntyre on page 148 (visible header) is outlining what is at stake in the confrontation between the three rival traditions — encyclopaedist, genealogist, and Thomistic — arguing that the intellectual issues dividing Nietzschean genealogy from the Thomistic tradition cannot be resolved by simply bracketing questions about ideological distortions and errors. The page appears to be setting up the conditions under which genuine rational enquiry across traditions is possible, specifically what rhetorical and argumentative resources would need to be deployed. MacIntyre is pushing toward the claim that the Thomistic tradition has a structural advantage because it can account for the failures of rival traditions from within its own framework.

MacIntyre argues that with Aquinas, the unified tradition of systematic philosophical enquiry reaching from Plato through the Islamic commentators achieves its highest point and then largely disappears from view — Thomistic commentary becomes a marginal, eclectic activity rather than a sustained cooperative project. He anticipates the objection that he is imposing a retrospective unity on what was in fact a heterogeneous, fragmented history of enquiry, and responds that all successful enquiry is necessarily written retrospectively — the history of physics provides the analogy. A tradition of enquiry characteristically bears within itself an always-open-to-revision history of itself.

MacIntyre argues that there is no 'single neutral nonpartisan history' of philosophy — the very shape of philosophical history depends on which tradition's standpoint one occupies, making rival histories genuinely incommensurable. He illustrates this with Aquinas: his account of Aquinas as the culmination of Augustinian and Aristotelian traditions is not how Aquinas was understood by his contemporaries, who found him eccentric and at odds with thirteenth-century orthodoxy. The remarkable historical fact, MacIntyre insists, is not Aquinas's condemnation in 1277 but his repeated revival and eventual canonization despite institutional resistance.

MacIntyre examines how the medieval reception of Aristotelianism was piecemeal and unsystematic, producing ad hoc revisions rather than coherent synthesis, with Duns Scotus emerging as the most distinguished anti-Thomistic figure. The central contrast developed is between Aquinas and Scotus on the telos of human beings: Aquinas argues that perfect happiness cannot be had in this life and that rational beings naturally seek it elsewhere, while Scotus contends that natural reason can know nothing of any life but this one, and that the imperfections Aquinas identifies as pointing beyond this life are merely the imperfections of embodied, mortal existence. This sets up a deeper theological disagreement about whether an essentially embodied mind — whose natural self-understanding is as mortal — can grasp the soul's immortality or the resurrection without revelation.

MacIntyre argues that Aquinas's deep integration of Aristotelian hylomorphism into his account of soul-body unity represents a more radical departure from Augustinianism than Scotus could accept. For Aquinas, a human being is not a soul that merely uses a body but a body that has a soul, making human experience essentially bodily and the mind 'radically incomplete' without sensory encounter with singulars. This Thomistic position provoked institutional Franciscan backlash — de la Mare's 1279 Correctorium and the 1282 prohibition on copying the Summa — setting the stage for Scotus's rival assertion of the soul's independence from bodily experience.

MacIntyre contrasts Aquinas's Aristotelian account of practical reason — where the will operates dependently on the intellect's apprehension of the genuine good — with the Augustinian tradition's unconditional primacy of will over intellect, a move Scotus inherits and radicalizes. The core argument is that once Scotus rejects Aristotle's psychology of will-intellect integration, the only remaining framework for moral obligation becomes divine command, and the 'ought' of 'one ought to obey God' collapses into the same 'ought' as practical reasoning generally — a conceptual flattening with enormous consequences. MacIntyre is showing how Scotus's voluntarism, born from a specific historical rupture, generates the distinctively modern problem of grounding moral obligation independently of rational apprehension of the good.

MacIntyre argues that Scotus's reconceptualization of the will as free and the intellect as practically inert generates a distinctively new 'ought' of moral obligation — one unknown to Aristotle — grounded not in the pursuit of the good but in the command of another. This move creates the problems that will define modern moral philosophy (Occam through Kant): why obey the command, and whether we can judge God's goodness by the same standard that grounds the obligation. In parallel, Scotus's doctrine of the soul's immediate intuitive knowledge of singulars transforms the metaphysical relationship between particular and universal, generating a new problematic for emerging academic metaphysics.

MacIntyre argues that the Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis of enquiry could not be accommodated within the existing curricular categories of either theology or the arts faculty at the University of Paris, because its scope and method made theological and philosophical enquiry inseparable. The outcome was an enlargement of the Arts curriculum to include moral, natural, and metaphysical philosophy, making philosophy the dominant academic discipline — but at the cost of theology's eventual isolation and unimportance. MacIntyre implies this institutional victory for philosophy carried the seeds of its own later crisis, a pattern academic philosophy would re-enact in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.

MacIntyre argues that while fourteenth- and fifteenth-century physics and astronomy were 'completed in being defeated' — absorbed into new frameworks via retrospective rational narrative — medieval moral philosophy and metaphysics dissolved into nothing: variety and heterogeneity rather than culminating achievement. This dissolution gave rise to academic philosophy as an institutionalized, professionalized university discipline, a 'scholasticism' with roots in 18th-19th century Scotland, Germany, and France, fully embodied in contemporary American academic culture. The marks of this institutionalized philosophy include high levels of professional skill, logical and conceptual technique, and admission only of those who master these techniques — a formation that structurally excludes the kinds of moral enquiry MacIntyre wants to recover.

MacIntyre characterizes the type of philosophy associated with defeated or continuing traditions by four features: (1) bureaucratically sanctioned standards and licensed professional skills, (2) problems that persist because they are never definitively solved but only reformulated, (3) recurrent and ineliminable basic disagreements due to lack of shared evaluative standards, and (4) practitioners who bring extra-philosophical commitments (ideological weltanschauungen) to the enquiry that the philosophy itself cannot adjudicate. The fourth characteristic explains why disagreement is ineliminable: these external standpoints (Tolstoyan morality, Bloomsbury aestheticism, scientific materialism, medieval theology) cannot be validated by the philosophical tradition itself, yet they shape what problems are acknowledged. This sets up MacIntyre's deeper point that tradition-constituted enquiry cannot be understood from a tradition-neutral standpoint.

MacIntyre argues that academic philosophy — whether medieval or modern — makes no genuine progress, because rival philosophical positions merely replace and displace one another without any overall directedness or teleological development emerging. He then turns to twentieth-century analytic philosophy, arguing it has condemned itself to repeat the history of late medieval Scholastic philosophy, each illuminating the other's structural failures. As evidence, he examines the Cambridge History of Later Medieval Philosophy, arguing that the encyclopaedic format itself enacts the Encyclopaedist error: assigning sovereignty over the past by measuring it against present standards of judgment.

MacIntyre critiques the Cambridge History of Later Medieval Philosophy for organizing its editorial perspective around 'recent analytic philosophy' as the standard of value, thereby distorting medieval thought by severing individual theses from the wholes they belong to. He argues this editorial stance makes it impossible to understand Aquinas's unified enquiry — the very unity that Aquinas, his Franciscan contemporaries, and his Averroist contemporaries shared but which Occam explicitly rejected, fracturing enquiry into irreducibly multiple and heterogeneous parts. The page thus traces the historical moment when the unity of moral-metaphysical enquiry collapsed, with Occam as the pivotal figure.

MacIntyre argues that the later Middle Ages, shaped by its institutional structures and modes of thought, was fundamentally unable to engage with Aquinas's Summa as a unified whole — a condition Aquinas himself had tried to remedy by writing the Summa to place moral enquiry within a systematic theological context. After Aquinas's death, the Dominican order reaffirmed his teaching but the tradition of commentary upon him became increasingly independent of late medieval philosophical mainstream, with parts of the Summa (especially the Secunda Secundae) being copied and studied in isolation from the whole — ironically inverting Aquinas's own integrative intention. This fragmentation of the Summa's reception becomes MacIntyre's evidence that institutional and curricular structures can defeat even the most architectonically unified moral project.

MacIntyre traces the fragmentation of Thomistic moral enquiry in the later Middle Ages: the Summa was dismembered by copyists and read piecemeal, Buridan's commentary on the Nicomachean Ethics exemplified a growing divorce between theoretical and practical philosophy, and Occam's voluntarist theory of obligation (evil as nothing other than acting against a divine command) displaced the Aristotelian-Thomistic synthesis. The result was the multiplication of moral standpoints independent of and hostile to Aristotle and Aquinas, including entirely new theories of natural rights that have no room within either an Aristotelian or Thomistic framework.

MacIntyre argues that Aquinas and Dante, as Augustinians and Aristotelians, tested legitimate government by the degree to which it secures justice, but by the early fourteenth century this appeal to justice was displaced by appeals to individual rights (ius), understood as a faculty entitling every creature to exert its abilities — rights now claimable without invoking shared conceptions of the common good. This shift coincides with the rise of classical rhetoric (via Petrarch and Cicero) as a rival to moral philosophy, and with the practice of casuistry expanding especially in economic life, eroding unconditional prohibitions like usury. The net effect is that the Thomistic framework, in which rights were embedded in a thick account of common good and virtue, becomes displaced by a thinner, more individualist and rhetorical moral culture.

MacIntyre argues that the thirteenth-century confrontation between Averroist Aristotelianism and Augustinianism produced two distinct outcomes: first, Aquinas's constructive dialectical synthesis, which had the capacity to direct enquiry further; and second, the subsequent university curriculum's marginalization of Aquinas's systematic thought, which excluded serious engagement with it and instead encountered it only as discrete theses. A third mode of repudiation is then introduced through Meister Eckhart, a Dominican thinker whose influence derived precisely from his antithetical, non-Thomistic use of materials drawn from Aquinas, representing the conceptual and moral pluralism of late medieval philosophy. This sets up MacIntyre's broader claim that the synthetic mode of Thomistic enquiry largely disappeared as a recognized tradition, surviving only in fragmentary or distorted form until the sixteenth-century Scholastic revival.

MacIntyre argues that Heidegger's rejection of Aquinas was not a misunderstanding but rather a rejection of the entire Platonic-Aristotelian tradition's conceptualization of being — yet ironically, what drove that rejection was precisely what made Heidegger most akin to Meister Eckhart. MacIntyre then pivots to Eckhart's sermons (not his philosophical works) as the relevant texts, arguing that in them we find an anticipation of Heidegger's thesis that being speaks to us through language rather than us using language to speak of being, and that conceptualization, systematic enquiry, and genre conventions become barriers to openness to being. This sets up a contrast between the Thomistic tradition's analogical predication — which acknowledges what lies beyond concepts while still requiring conceptualization — and the Eckhartian-Heideggerian move to non-argumentative, non-conceptual modes of speech.

MacIntyre argues that Meister Eckhart's preaching represented a deliberate disintegration of the Thomist idiom of rational discourse, replacing it with a mode of speech unconstrained by logic, analogy, or scientia — one that claimed access to a 'noble power of the soul' beyond reason. This move was not merely heterodox theology but a structural separation of preaching from rational theology as the Dominicans had understood it, effectively creating preaching as a self-sufficient cultural artifact and social practice. The page sets up Eckhart as an early exemplar of the 'genealogist' type — someone who abandons tradition's rational constraints in favor of a discourse that claims its own sovereign warrant.

MacIntyre argues that philosophers stand in two distinct relationships to society: as active participants in public rational debate (type a), or as professionalized academic elites whose technical vocabulary excludes the broader public (type b). When philosophy becomes professionalized, those excluded from the elite discourse respond not with counter-arguments but with rhetorical effectiveness and manipulation. This dynamic explains why doctrines promoted by rhetorically effective popular preachers function as untouchable talismans — their purpose is not rational persuasion but inoculation against rational accountability.

MacIntyre argues that Eckhart's 'emphatic' rhetorical style represents a divorce between rhetorical effectiveness and rational argumentation, which violates the thirteenth-century Dominican ideal—exemplified by Aquinas—that the homily should be the end-product of philosophical and theological education. He then broadens this into a cultural-epistemological claim: cultures are defined not only by their achievements but by what they structurally cannot see or acknowledge, and the failure to understand Aquinas in the late Middle Ages is a paradigm case of such culturally-induced blindness—one that may persist into the present.

MacIntyre opens Chapter VIII by identifying the deepest point of conflict among the three rival traditions: not merely how moral enquiry should be conducted, but whether rational persons can even agree on the standards and criteria for evaluating competing claims. The Ninth Edition encyclopaedists presupposed that all rational persons can resolve disagreements on questions of standards and method, while Nietzschean genealogy denies this entirely. MacIntyre then poses the question of why the Ninth Edition standpoint deserves continued serious engagement, given that nobody now explicitly holds it, and promises three reasons why dismissing it would be a mistake.

MacIntyre argues that even after the collapse of the Encyclopaedist project, three residual legacies persist: (1) academic institutions still operate as if Enlightenment coherence were intact, (2) the 'ghosts of the Ninth Edition' haunt the contemporary academy and must be exorcized, and (3) most critically, the encyclopaedic belief in the universal commensurability of rational standpoints continues to dominate curriculum design — the assumption that any text from any culture can be translated and made intelligible to any student. He notes that while some philosophers defend this via Donald Davidson's arguments, the dominant academic culture holds this belief not because of philosophical argument but as an unexamined presupposition.

MacIntyre argues that contemporary academic practice differs crucially from its Enlightenment predecessors by accepting only a 'weak' conception of rationality — one that tolerates the coexistence of widely divergent views without requiring conclusive resolution. Whereas Enlightenment rationality (epitomized by the Ninth Edition of the Encyclopaedia Britannica) held that rational debate could always, if properly conducted, yield definitive truth, contemporary academia has abandoned this aspiration. The deeper reason for this abandonment is that any standard strong enough to adjudicate between rival frameworks would itself have to be embedded in some particular theoretical and conceptual scheme — there is no view from nowhere.

MacIntyre argues on this page that when two large-scale theoretical standpoints are systematically at odds, it may appear there is no rational way to adjudicate between them — a conclusion Nietzschean genealogists embrace. But he counters that Aquinas demonstrably refuted this conclusion in the thirteenth century by engaging Augustinianism and Aristotelianism on their own internal terms, showing each tradition's inadequacies from within. The Thomist strategy — raising critical questions for a rival tradition in that tradition's own terms, not one's own — is thus the model for how enquiry across rival frameworks can proceed rationally, and MacIntyre extends this as a challenge to both encyclopaedists and genealogists.
